text: The surgeon begins the supra-azygus and infra-azygus mobilization of the azygus vein by cutting the parietal pleura above and below the vein using monopolar scissors held in the left and right hands. The azygus vein is then ligated and cut to facilitate the recurrent laryngeal lymph node dissection.

The assistant holds up the azygus vein to expose the prevascular fascia, indicated by the right black arrow. The surgeon dissects within the prevascular fascia, with the aorta and the arch of the aorta visible behind.

The surgeon begins the left recurrent laryngeal dissection by identifying the prevascular fascia, visible in the PIP mode. The thoracic duct is seen near the left border of the esophagus, requiring careful handling to prevent injury. As the dissection progresses along the prevascular fascia, the ascending aorta and the arch of the aorta are fully exposed.

The thoracic duct is visible along the aorta and its adventitia, with the glistening white surface of the aorta fully exposed. The prevascular fascia is completely exposed up to the arch of the aorta, where the left recurrent laryngeal nerve hooks underneath. The left vagus nerve is identified and cut in the infra-bronchial region.

The surgeon identifies the visceral fascia between the esophagus and the trachea. The trachea is retracted using cherry forceps, a cherry retractor, and a fan retractor, performing a maneuver known as the tracheal dip. The dissection proceeds carefully along the left border of the trachea.

The surgeon carefully dissects along the left lateral border of the trachea, following the visceral fascia marked by a white arrow in the PIP mode. The trachea is retracted towards the surgeon, and the visceral pleura is cut, revealing an avascular zone. The dissection proceeds parallel to the trachea, encompassing the left recurrent laryngeal lymph node packet shaded in green in the PIP mode.

The surgeon follows the visceral pleura to meet the visceral and prevascular fascia behind the esophagus, lifting the left recurrent laryngeal lymph node packet along with the left recurrent laryngeal nerve and esophagus. The dissection is performed using monopolar cautery in the avascular zone without additional energy sources.

The left recurrent laryngeal nerve is visible emerging from the lymph node packet, indicated by a black arrow in the PIP mode. Dissection is performed above and below the nerve, ensuring that lymph node tissue and lymphatics are included in the specimen along with the esophagus.

The fan retractor is used to retract the left main bronchus and trachea towards the surgeon, exposing the left recurrent laryngeal lymph nodal packet area. The esophagus is lifted up, and the assistant aids in exposure by lifting the esophagus and retracting the trachea down and towards the surgeon on the right side, revealing the lymph nodal tissue and lymph nodes along the esophagus.

The surgeon performs dissection within the lymph nodal packet, carefully pushing down the recurrent laryngeal nerve using the robotic right arm equipped with monopolar scissors. The dissection is conducted without using energy sources near the nerve to prevent nerve palsy, relying primarily on bare scissors for precision.

The tracheoesophageal groove is visible, cleared of fibro fatty and lymph nodal tissue. The surgeon cuts the prevascular fascia, revealing the thoracic duct in the background. The dissection continues behind the esophagus towards the aorta and left pleura to excise the prevascular fascia, indicated by a white arrow in the PIP mode.

The surgeon cuts the visceral fascia and prevascular fascia to expose the aorta beneath and behind the esophagus. The dissection continues down to the infracoronal region, revealing the right main bronchus and the subcoronal lymph nodal packet. The dissection is performed close to the inferior borders of the right and left main bronchi.

The surgeon exposes the carina and left main bronchus, performing subcarinal dissection. A small twig of tissue is cut, and the subcarinal lymph node is lifted from the pericardium. The subcarinal area is cleared of fibro fatty and lymphatic tissue, with minimal bleeding visible due to precise dissection along anatomical planes.
